pages.title: Vanishing criteria, coherence conditions
pages.html: VII Vanishing criteria, coherence conditions for the sheaves \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{i}_{Y}(F, G)\)

blocks.label: VII
blocks.html: VII Vanishing criteria, coherence conditions for the sheaves \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{i}_{Y}(F, G)\)

pages.title: Study for \(i < n\)
pages.html:
Study for \(i < n\)

Let us prove a lemma:

Lemma 1.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(G\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Suppose that for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) with support contained in \(Y\), we have:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{n-1}(F, G) = 0. \]

Then for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) and every closed set \(Z\) of \(X\) such that \(Y \supset \operatorname{Supp} F \cap Z\), we have

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z(F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)). \]

We first note that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F, G) = \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_{Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F}(F, G). \]

(trivial, cf. Exposé VI). We first carry out the proof for \(Z \!=\! X\), hence \(\operatorname{Supp} F \!\subset\! Y\). The functor

\[ F \rightsquigarrow \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n(F, G), \]

defined on the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules with support contained in \(Y\), is left exact. By virtue of (IV 1.2), it is representable by

\[ I = \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{k+1}, G), \]

where \(\mathscr{I}\) is the ideal of definition of Y. Now, by (II 6), we know that:

\[ \underline{H}^n_Y(G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{k+1}, G). \]

Whence the conclusion if \( Z= X\). Again by (VI 2.3) we know that:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, G), \]

where \(\mathscr{J}\) is the ideal of definition of Z. The support of \(F/\mathscr{J}^{k+1}F\) is contained in \(Y\) if \(Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\); by what we have just proved, we therefore have:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)). \]

It remains to show that the natural homomorphism:

\[ \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y (G)), \]

is an isomorphism when \(Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\). Now \(X\) can be covered by noetherian affine open sets; we are thus reduced to the case where \(X\) is noetherian affine; then \(F(X)\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_X(X)\)-Module of finite type and \(\operatorname{Supp} \underline{H}^n_Y(G) \subset Y\). Hence every homomorphism \(u: F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{H}^n_Y (G) (X)\) is annihilated by a power of \(\mathscr{I}\), hence by a power of \(\mathscr{J}\), Q.E.D.

Proposition 1.2

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(G\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module and \(n\) an integer. Whatever \(Z\) and \(S\), closed subsets of \(X\) such that \(Z \cap S = Y\), the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\underline{H}^i_Y (G) = 0\) if \(i<n\);

  2. there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), with support \(S\), such that:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Z (F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i<n; \]

  3. for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) with support contained in \(S\) (i.e. \(\operatorname{Supp} F \cap Z\) \(=\) \(\operatorname{Supp} F \cap Y)\), we have:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_{Z}(F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i < n; \]

  4. for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), we have:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i < n. \]

Moreover, if they are satisfied, then for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module F and every closed subset \(Z'\) of \(X\) such that \(Z' \cap \operatorname{Supp} F = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), we have isomorphisms:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_{Z} (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Y (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y (G)). \]
Proof▾

We argue by induction. The proposition is trivial for \(n < 0\). Suppose it proved for \(n < q\). If one of the conditions is satisfied for \(n = q\), and for two subsets \(Z\) and \(S\) as stated, then by the induction hypothesis we have, for every closed set \(Z'\) of \(X\) and every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(Z' \cap \operatorname{Supp} F = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{1.1}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q-1}_{Z'} (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^{q-1}_Y (G)) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q-1}_Y (F, G). \]

Hence:

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iv), since we take \(Z' = Y\) in (1.1);

(iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii), since we take \(Z' = Z\) in (1.1);

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii), since we take \(F = \mathscr{O}_S\);

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), since we take \(Z' = Z\) in (1.1); whence \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}_Y^{q-1} (G)) = 0\); we then note that:

\[ \operatorname{Supp} \underline{H}_Y^{q-1} (G) \subset Y = Z \cap S \subset S = \operatorname{Supp} F, \]

and we apply the following lemma:

Lemma 1.3

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, let \(P\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, and let \(H\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module such that:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (P, H) = 0 \text{ and } \operatorname{Supp} P \supset \operatorname{Supp} H. \]

Then \(H = 0\).

It suffices to prove the lemma when \(X\) is affine, since the affine open sets form a base of the topology of \(X\) and the hypotheses are preserved under restriction to an open set. Now, in this case, we are reduced to a problem about \(A\)-modules, where \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). We apply the formula (valid under the sole hypothesis that \(M\) is of finite type):

\[ \operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A (P, H) = \operatorname{Supp} P \cap \operatorname{Ass} H; \]

We know that \(\operatorname{Ass} H \subset \operatorname{Supp} H \subset \operatorname{Supp} P\) and that \(\operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A (P, H) = \emptyset\); hence \(\operatorname{Ass} H = \emptyset\), hence \(H = 0\).

To complete the proof of the proposition, it remains to note that (iv) allows us to apply (1.1).

Q.E.D.
Corollary 1.4

Let \(G\) be a coherent and Cohen-Macaulay \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, let \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\). The conditions of (1.2) are equivalent to:

  1. (v)

    \(\operatorname{codim}(Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} G, \operatorname{Supp} G) \geqslant n\).

Let us first recall that an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module is said to be Cohen-Macaulay if, for every \(x \in X\), the fibre \(G_x\) is a Cohen-Macaulay \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\)-module, i.e. for every \(x \in S = \operatorname{Supp} G\) we have:

\[ \tag{1.2} \operatorname{prof} G_x = \dim G_x = \dim \mathscr{O}_{S, x}. \]

By (III 3.3), condition (i) of (1.2) is equivalent to:

\[ \tag{1.3} \operatorname{prof}_Y G = \inf_{x\in Y} \operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n, \]

hence also to:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y G = \inf_{x \in Y \cap S} \operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n; \]

since the depth of a zero module is infinite.

Now, by definition:

\[ \operatorname{codim} (Y \cap S, S) = \inf_{x \in S \cap Y} \dim \mathscr{O}_{S, x}, \]

whence the conclusion, by applying formula (1.2)).

We shall now prove a result that allows us to deduce the coherence conditions we have in view from certain vanishing criteria.

Lemma 1.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. Let \(T^{\ast}\) be a contravariant exact \(\partial\)-functor, defined on the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules, with values in the category of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(i \in \mathbf{Z}\). Suppose that, for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module with support contained in \(Y\), \(T^i F\) and \(T^{i-1} F\) are coherent. Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. For \(T^i F\) to be coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(T^i F''\) be so, where we have set:

\[ F'' = F / \Gamma_Y (F). \]

Indeed, \(F' = \Gamma_Y (F)\) is coherent since \(X\) is locally noetherian; the exact cohomology sequence of \(T^{\ast}\) then gives:

\[ T^{i-1}F' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F' \]

where the extreme terms are coherent, whence the conclusion.

Lemma 1.6

If \(F\) and \(G\) are coherent, and if \(\operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\), then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent.

Indeed, \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i (F, G)\); this holds over any ringed space \(X\), moreover: if \(Z\) is a closed set containing \(Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Z (F, G)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) (cf. Exposé VI).

Proposition 1.7

3mmSuppose \(F\) and \(G\) coherent and set \(\operatorname{Supp} F = S\), \(S' = \overline{S \cap (X-Y)}\). Suppose that, for every \(x \in Y \cap S'\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n\); then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent for \(i < n\).

Indeed, (1.6) allows us to apply (1.5) to \(T^{\ast}(F) = \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{\ast}_Y (F, G)\). Setting \(F'' = F/ \Gamma_Y (F)\), we see that \(\operatorname{Supp} F'' = S'\), hence \(T^i F'' = 0\) for \(i < n\), thanks to (1.2) and to (III 3.1), whence the conclusion.

blocks.html: Study for \(i < n\)
Let us prove a lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(G\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Suppose that for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) with support contained in \(Y\), we have:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{n-1}(F, G) = 0. \]

Then for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) and every closed set \(Z\) of \(X\) such that \(Y \supset \operatorname{Supp} F \cap Z\), we have

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z(F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)). \]
We first note that
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Z(F, G) = \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_{Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F}(F, G). \]
(trivial, cf. Exposé VI). We first carry out the proof for \(Z \!=\! X\), hence \(\operatorname{Supp} F \!\subset\! Y\). The functor
\[ F \rightsquigarrow \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n(F, G), \]
defined on the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules with support contained in \(Y\), is left exact. By virtue of (IV 1.2), it is representable by
\[ I = \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{k+1}, G), \]
where \(\mathscr{I}\) is the ideal of definition of Y. Now, by (II 6), we know that:
\[ \underline{H}^n_Y(G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{k+1}, G). \]
Whence the conclusion if \( Z= X\). Again by (VI 2.3) we know that:
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, G), \]

where \(\mathscr{J}\) is the ideal of definition of Z. The support of \(F/\mathscr{J}^{k+1}F\) is contained in \(Y\) if \(Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\); by what we have just proved, we therefore have:
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Z (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)). \]
It remains to show that the natural homomorphism:

\[ \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F/\mathscr{J}^{k + 1}F, \underline{H}^n_Y(G)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y (G)), \]

is an isomorphism when \(Z \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\). Now \(X\) can be covered by noetherian affine open sets; we are thus reduced to the case where \(X\) is noetherian affine; then \(F(X)\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_X(X)\)-Module of finite type and \(\operatorname{Supp} \underline{H}^n_Y(G) \subset Y\). Hence every homomorphism \(u: F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{H}^n_Y (G) (X)\) is annihilated by a power of \(\mathscr{I}\), hence by a power of \(\mathscr{J}\), Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(G\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module and \(n\) an integer. Whatever \(Z\) and \(S\), closed subsets of \(X\) such that \(Z \cap S = Y\), the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\underline{H}^i_Y (G) = 0\) if \(i<n\);

  2. there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), with support \(S\), such that:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Z (F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i<n; \]

  3. for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) with support contained in \(S\) (i.e. \(\operatorname{Supp} F \cap Z\) \(=\) \(\operatorname{Supp} F \cap Y)\), we have:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_{Z}(F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i < n; \]

  4. for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\), we have:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) = 0 \text{ if } i < n. \]

Moreover, if they are satisfied, then for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module F and every closed subset \(Z'\) of \(X\) such that \(Z' \cap \operatorname{Supp} F = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), we have isomorphisms:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_{Z} (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Y (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^n_Y (G)). \]

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

We argue by induction. The proposition is trivial for \(n < 0\). Suppose it proved for \(n < q\). If one of the conditions is satisfied for \(n = q\), and for two subsets \(Z\) and \(S\) as stated, then by the induction hypothesis we have, for every closed set \(Z'\) of \(X\) and every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(Z' \cap \operatorname{Supp} F = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{1.1}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q-1}_{Z'} (F, G) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}^{q-1}_Y (G)) \approx \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q-1}_Y (F, G). \]

Hence:

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iv), since we take \(Z' = Y\) in (1.1);

(iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii), since we take \(Z' = Z\) in (1.1);

(iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii), since we take \(F = \mathscr{O}_S\);

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), since we take \(Z' = Z\) in (1.1); whence \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (F, \underline{H}_Y^{q-1} (G)) = 0\); we then note that:

\[ \operatorname{Supp} \underline{H}_Y^{q-1} (G) \subset Y = Z \cap S \subset S = \operatorname{Supp} F, \]

and we apply the following lemma:

Lemma 1.3

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, let \(P\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, and let \(H\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module such that:

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits (P, H) = 0 \text{ and } \operatorname{Supp} P \supset \operatorname{Supp} H. \]

Then \(H = 0\).

It suffices to prove the lemma when \(X\) is affine, since the affine open sets form a base of the topology of \(X\) and the hypotheses are preserved under restriction to an open set. Now, in this case, we are reduced to a problem about \(A\)-modules, where \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). We apply the formula (valid under the sole hypothesis that \(M\) is of finite type):

\[ \operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A (P, H) = \operatorname{Supp} P \cap \operatorname{Ass} H; \]

We know that \(\operatorname{Ass} H \subset \operatorname{Supp} H \subset \operatorname{Supp} P\) and that \(\operatorname{Ass} \operatorname{Hom}_A (P, H) = \emptyset\); hence \(\operatorname{Ass} H = \emptyset\), hence \(H = 0\).

To complete the proof of the proposition, it remains to note that (iv) allows us to apply (1.1).

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

Let \(G\) be a coherent and Cohen-Macaulay \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, let \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\). The conditions of (1.2) are equivalent to:

  1. (v)

    \(\operatorname{codim}(Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} G, \operatorname{Supp} G) \geqslant n\).

Let us first recall that an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module is said to be Cohen-Macaulay if, for every \(x \in X\), the fibre \(G_x\) is a Cohen-Macaulay \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\)-module, i.e. for every \(x \in S = \operatorname{Supp} G\) we have:
\[ \tag{1.2} \operatorname{prof} G_x = \dim G_x = \dim \mathscr{O}_{S, x}. \]
By (III 3.3), condition (i) of (1.2) is equivalent to:
\[ \tag{1.3} \operatorname{prof}_Y G = \inf_{x\in Y} \operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n, \]
hence also to:
\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y G = \inf_{x \in Y \cap S} \operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n; \]
since the depth of a zero module is infinite.
Now, by definition:
\[ \operatorname{codim} (Y \cap S, S) = \inf_{x \in S \cap Y} \dim \mathscr{O}_{S, x}, \]
whence the conclusion, by applying formula (1.2)).
We shall now prove a result that allows us to deduce the coherence conditions we have in view from certain vanishing criteria.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. Let \(T^{\ast}\) be a contravariant exact \(\partial\)-functor, defined on the category of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules, with values in the category of \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(i \in \mathbf{Z}\). Suppose that, for every coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module with support contained in \(Y\), \(T^i F\) and \(T^{i-1} F\) are coherent. Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. For \(T^i F\) to be coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(T^i F''\) be so, where we have set:

\[ F'' = F / \Gamma_Y (F). \]
Indeed, \(F' = \Gamma_Y (F)\) is coherent since \(X\) is locally noetherian; the exact cohomology sequence of \(T^{\ast}\) then gives:

\[ T^{i-1}F' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T^i F' \]
where the extreme terms are coherent, whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

If \(F\) and \(G\) are coherent, and if \(\operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\), then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent.

Indeed, \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i (F, G)\); this holds over any ringed space \(X\), moreover: if \(Z\) is a closed set containing \(Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F\), then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i_Z (F, G)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}} olimits^i_Y (F, G)\) (cf. Exposé VI).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7

3mmSuppose \(F\) and \(G\) coherent and set \(\operatorname{Supp} F = S\), \(S' = \overline{S \cap (X-Y)}\). Suppose that, for every \(x \in Y \cap S'\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} G_x \geqslant n\); then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent for \(i < n\).

Indeed, (1.6) allows us to apply (1.5) to \(T^{\ast}(F) = \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{\ast}_Y (F, G)\). Setting \(F'' = F/ \Gamma_Y (F)\), we see that \(\operatorname{Supp} F'' = S'\), hence \(T^i F'' = 0\) for \(i < n\), thanks to (1.2) and to (III 3.1), whence the conclusion.

pages.title: Study for \(i>n\)
pages.html:
2 Study for \(i>n\)

Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, that is, one all of whose local rings are regular. Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(F\) and \(G\) be two coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. Set \(S = \operatorname{Supp} F\), \(S' = \overline{S \cap (X - Y)}\). Set:

\begin{align*} m& = \sup_{x \in Y \cap S} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x},\\ n &= \sup_{x \in Y \cap S'} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}; \end{align*}

we have \(n \leqslant m\).

Proposition 2.1

In the situation described above, we have:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) = 0\) if \(i > m\),

  2. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent if \(i > n\).

Let us first remark that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent for all \(i\) when \(\operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\). Moreover, setting \(F'' = F/\Gamma_Y(F)\) as above, one sees that \(\operatorname{Supp} F'' = S'\), hence (2) follows from (1) and from (1.3).

To prove (1), we first remark that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i (F/\mathscr{J}^k F, G), \]

where \(\mathscr{J}\) is the ideal of definition of \(Y\). On the other hand, it follows from theorem 4.2.2. of ( A. Grothendieck, Sur quelques points d'algèbre homologique, Tohoku Mathematical Journal 1957) that the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits\) commute with the formation of stalks, at least when \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme and when the first argument is coherent; since the same holds for inductive limits, one finds isomorphisms:

\[ (\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G))_x \approx \varinjlim_{k} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}} ((F/\mathscr{J}^k F)_x, G_x) \]

for all \(x \in X\). Since \(\operatorname{Supp} \operatorname{Ext}^i_Y (F, G) \subset S \cap Y\), it suffices, in order to conclude, to remark that \(x \in Y \cap S\) implies \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \leqslant m\), hence:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^i_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}} ((F/\mathscr{J}^k F)_x, G_x) = 0 \text { if } i > m, \]

since the global cohomological dimension of a regular local ring is equal to its dimension1.

Let \(X\) be a loc. noetherian prescheme; for every subset \(P\) of \(X\), set:

\[ D(P) = \left\{ \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p} \mid p \in P \right\}. \]
Lemma 2.2

If \(P\) is the underlying space of a connected subprescheme of \(A\), \(D(P)\) is an interval.

Indeed, let \(a\) and \(B\) belong to \(D(P)\), corresponding to points \(p\) and \(q\) of \(P\). Let us show that there exists a sequence of points of \(P\): \((p = p_1, \dots, p_n = q)\) such that, for \(1 \leqslant i < n \), one has \(\left| \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p_i} - \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p_{i+1}} \right| = 1\); it will follow that D(P) contains the interval \([p, q]\). For this, one remarks that \(p\) and \(q\) can be joined by a sequence of irreducible components of \(P\), such that two successive components meet. One is reduced to the case where \(p\) is the generic point of an irreducible component \(Q\) of \(P\), and where \(q \in Q\) and hence \(q \supset p\), as ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_q\), where it is trivial from the definition of dimension.

Proposition 2.3

Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Let \(P = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \cap (X - Y)\). Let \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\), and suppose that \(n \not\in D(P)\). Then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Y (F, \mathscr{O}_X)\) is coherent.

The conclusion is local and the hypotheses are preserved under restriction to an open set. Now \(P\) is closed hence locally noetherian, hence locally connected; one may therefore suppose \(X\) affine and noetherian, and \(P\) connected. Set \(D(P) = [a, b[\), which is licit by the preceding lemma; if \(n> b\), one concludes by (2.1); if \(n < a\), one has \(n < \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} = \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) for all \(x \in P\), and one concludes by (1.7).

blocks.html: 2 Study for \(i>n\)

Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, that is, one all of whose local rings are regular. Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(F\) and \(G\) be two coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Modules. Set \(S = \operatorname{Supp} F\), \(S' = \overline{S \cap (X - Y)}\). Set:
\begin{align*} m& = \sup_{x \in Y \cap S} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x},\\ n &= \sup_{x \in Y \cap S'} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}; \end{align*}
we have \(n \leqslant m\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

In the situation described above, we have:

  1. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) = 0\) if \(i > m\),

  2. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent if \(i > n\).

Let us first remark that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G)\) is coherent for all \(i\) when \(\operatorname{Supp} F \subset Y\). Moreover, setting \(F'' = F/\Gamma_Y(F)\) as above, one sees that \(\operatorname{Supp} F'' = S'\), hence (2) follows from (1) and from (1.3).
To prove (1), we first remark that
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G) \approx \varinjlim_{k} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i (F/\mathscr{J}^k F, G), \]

where \(\mathscr{J}\) is the ideal of definition of \(Y\). On the other hand, it follows from theorem 4.2.2. of ( A. Grothendieck, Sur quelques points d'algèbre homologique, Tohoku Mathematical Journal 1957) that the \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits\) commute with the formation of stalks, at least when \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme and when the first argument is coherent; since the same holds for inductive limits, one finds isomorphisms:
\[ (\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^i_Y (F, G))_x \approx \varinjlim_{k} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}} ((F/\mathscr{J}^k F)_x, G_x) \]

for all \(x \in X\). Since \(\operatorname{Supp} \operatorname{Ext}^i_Y (F, G) \subset S \cap Y\), it suffices, in order to conclude, to remark that \(x \in Y \cap S\) implies \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} \leqslant m\), hence:
\[ \operatorname{Ext}^i_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}} ((F/\mathscr{J}^k F)_x, G_x) = 0 \text { if } i > m, \]
since the global cohomological dimension of a regular local ring is equal to its dimension1.
Let \(X\) be a loc. noetherian prescheme; for every subset \(P\) of \(X\), set:
\[ D(P) = \left\{ \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p} \mid p \in P \right\}. \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

If \(P\) is the underlying space of a connected subprescheme of \(A\), \(D(P)\) is an interval.

Indeed, let \(a\) and \(B\) belong to \(D(P)\), corresponding to points \(p\) and \(q\) of \(P\). Let us show that there exists a sequence of points of \(P\): \((p = p_1, \dots, p_n = q)\) such that, for \(1 \leqslant i < n \), one has \(\left| \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p_i} - \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, p_{i+1}} \right| = 1\); it will follow that D(P) contains the interval \([p, q]\). For this, one remarks that \(p\) and \(q\) can be joined by a sequence of irreducible components of \(P\), such that two successive components meet. One is reduced to the case where \(p\) is the generic point of an irreducible component \(Q\) of \(P\), and where \(q \in Q\) and hence \(q \supset p\), as ideals of \(\mathscr{O}_q\), where it is trivial from the definition of dimension.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let \(X\) be a regular locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Let \(P = Y \cap \operatorname{Supp} F \cap (X - Y)\). Let \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\), and suppose that \(n \not\in D(P)\). Then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^n_Y (F, \mathscr{O}_X)\) is coherent.

The conclusion is local and the hypotheses are preserved under restriction to an open set. Now \(P\) is closed hence locally noetherian, hence locally connected; one may therefore suppose \(X\) affine and noetherian, and \(P\) connected. Set \(D(P) = [a, b[\), which is licit by the preceding lemma; if \(n> b\), one concludes by (2.1); if \(n < a\), one has \(n < \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} = \operatorname{prof} \mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) for all \(x \in P\), and one concludes by (1.7).
